1: pseudoprophētai en tō laō — false prophets among the people (the Jewish prophetic tradition included false prophets from Moses' time — Deut 13; 18:20-22; the pattern recurs). pseudodidaskaloi en hymin — false teachers among you; the shift from prophētai to didaskaloi reflects the post-apostolic ecclesial structure where teaching rather than prophesy is the primary function of leadership. haireseis apōleias — destructive heresies/factions (hairesis = a chosen position, a school of thought, a faction — not yet the fully technical meaning of "heresy" but the beginning of the trajectory). ton agorasanta autous despotēn arnoumenoi — denying the Master who bought them (agorazō = to buy in the marketplace — the redemption/ransom vocabulary; the false teachers are within the redeemed community yet deny the sovereign who purchased them).
2: aselgeiais — their licentiousness/debauchery (aselgeia = conduct without restraint; the letter repeatedly pairs doctrinal error with sexual and social license — the two are not separable). hē hodos tēs alētheias blasphēmēthēsetai — the way of truth will be blasphemed (the early Christians described their movement as a hodos = way/road — Acts 9:2; 19:9,23; 24:14,22; the false teachers' conduct gives ammunition to those who slander the entire movement).
3: plastois logois hymas emporeuontai — with fabricated/molded words they will exploit you (plastos = molded, artificial — from plassō = to mold clay; the false teachers' words are artfully manufactured rather than faithfully received). emporeuontai = they will trade in you, exploit you for profit (emporos = merchant, trader — the false teachers' pastoral relationship is commercial exploitation). to krima ekpalai ouk argei kai hē apōleia autōn ou nystazei — their judgment from of old is not idle and their destruction does not slumber (argei = is idle, from argos = without work; the divine judgment may appear slow but it is never asleep).
4: angelōn hamartēsantōn ouk epheisato — he did not spare angels when they sinned (referring to Gen 6:1-4 and the Enochic tradition about the Watchers who transgressed divine boundaries). tartarōsas — having consigned to Tartarus (tartaroō = to send to Tartarus; the only NT use of this Greek mythological term; Tartarus was the deepest underworld in Greek mythology, below Hades — used here as a borrowing for the place of maximum divine imprisonment; this shows Peter's willingness to use culturally accessible vocabulary for theological purposes). seirais zophou tēresamenous — confined in chains of darkness for judgment.
5: archaion kosmon ouk epheisato — he did not spare the ancient world; Noah dikaiosynēs kēryka phylaxas — protecting Noah, a herald of righteousness (kēryx = a herald, one who makes public proclamation; the designation of Noah as a preacher is not in Genesis but in Second Temple Jewish tradition — Josephus Ant. 1.74; Philo — developing the biblical record of Noah's construction of the ark as an implicit proclamation). ogdoon — eighth (Noah plus seven others = eight; cf. 1 Pet 3:20).
6: Sodomon kai Gomorras poleis tephrōsas katastroph ē katekrinen — he condemned the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah, reducing them to ashes in a catastrophe (tephrōsas = having reduced to ash, from tephra = ash — a hapax in the NT; the word may reflect volcanic ash and destruction imagery). hypodeigma mellontōn asebein — an example for those who are going to be ungodly (the destruction of Sodom functions as an eschatological type: what happened to those cities prefigures the judgment of the ungodly in the last days).
7: dikaion Lōt kataponoumenon — righteous Lot being worn down/distressed (kataponeomai = to be exhausted by toil, worn down — only here in NT; Lot's righteousness is more emphasized in Second Temple tradition than in Genesis itself). hypo tēs tōn athesmōn en aselgeia anastrophēs — by the conduct in licentiousness of the lawless ones (athesmos = without law/divine ordinance, lawless).
8: blemati gar kai akoē ho dikaios — for by sight and hearing the righteous man (blemma = a look, a glance — a rare word for the act of seeing); enkatoikōn en autois hēmeran ex hēmeras — dwelling among them day by day; psychēn dikaian anomois ergois ebasanizen — was tormenting his righteous soul with lawless deeds (basanizō = to torture, test as on a touchstone; Lot's soul was on the rack of what he witnessed daily — a vivid image of the suffering of righteousness in a hostile environment).
9: oiden Kyrios eusebeois ek peirasmou ryesthai — the Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trial (oiden = he knows — the divine knowledge as the basis of the divine action; the summary principle of the three examples). adikous de eis hēmeran kriseōs kolazomenous tērein — and to hold the unrighteous for the day of judgment while being punished (the present passive participle kolazomenous suggests ongoing punishment during the holding — not merely detention but active discipline while awaiting final judgment).
10: malista de tous opisō sarkos en epithymia miasmou poreuomenous — especially those who walk behind the flesh in the desire of defilement (miasmos = pollution, defilement — the OT purity vocabulary applied to the false teachers' conduct). kyriotētos kataphronountas — despising lordship/dominion (kyriotēs = lordship, authority — possibly divine sovereignty or angelic authorities). tolmētai, authadeis, doxas ou tremousin blasphēmountes — audacious and self-willed, not trembling while blaspheming the glorious ones (authadēs = self-pleasing, self-willed — the vice of total self-referentiality).
11: hopou angeloi ischyi kai dynamei meizones ontes — whereas angels, greater in strength and power (ischys and dynamis are paired as complementary force-words — the natural/inherent capacity and the operative power). ou pherousin kat'autōn para Kyriō blasphēmon krisin — do not bring a slanderous judgment against them before the Lord (the angels who are mightier than the false teachers' targets nevertheless exercise restraint in accusation — implying the false teachers show a reckless arrogance that even angels avoid).
12: hōs aloga zōa physika gennēta eis halōsin kai phthoran — like unreasoning natural animals born for capture and destruction (alogos = without logos/reason; the false teachers act by instinct like animals bred to be caught and killed). en hois agnoousin blasphēmountes — blaspheming in matters they do not understand (agnoousin = they are ignorant of — the intellectual failure behind the arrogance: speaking authoritatively about what one does not know). en tē phthora autōn kai phthārēsontai — in their destruction they will be destroyed (wordplay on phthora — corruption/destruction — the same word as 1:4's corruption from which believers escape).
13: adikoumenoi misthon adikias — receiving as wages the wages of unrighteousness (adikia appears twice — the wordplay: the unrighteous thing they do produces unrighteous wages; a reaping-what-you-sow structure). hēdonēn hēgoumenoi tēn en hēmera tryphēn — counting delight in daytime revelry as pleasure (tryphē = luxurious/soft living, sensual pleasure; daytime carousing was specifically disreputable — nighttime drinking was conventional, but daytime drinking marked a person as beyond social norms). spiloi kai mōmoi — spots and blemishes (spilos = a stain; mōmos = a blemish, fault — the language of cultic impurity applied to social conduct).
14: ophthalmoūs mestous moichalidos — eyes full of adulteress (the metaphor is striking: the gaze itself is contaminated by the object of desire, as though adultery fills the very organ of perception). akatapastous hamartias — insatiable for sin (akatapaustos = without ceasing, unceasing — the incapacity to stop sinning). deleazontes psychas astēriktous — luring unstable souls (deleazō = to bait a trap, lure by bait — the same hunting/fishing term as Jas 1:14; astēriktos = unestablished, unstable — the opposite of those established in the present truth, 1:12). karatekhnimenoi pleonexia — hearts trained in greed (gymnasein = trained by exercise, like an athlete).
15: kataleipontes eutheian hodon eplanukenthēsan — having abandoned the straight way they have wandered (kataleipō = to leave behind, abandon — the decisive departure from the right path; planaomai = to wander, be led astray — the same root as planē, error/deception). tē hodō tou Balaam tou Beōr — the way of Balaam son of Beor; Balaam is the OT archetype of the prophet who uses his gift for personal financial gain (Num 22-24; 31:16); misthon adikias ēgapēsen — he loved the wages of unrighteousness.
16: elegxin de eschen idias paranomias — but he had a rebuke of his own transgression (elegxin = a conviction, a formal rebuke; paranomia = transgression against law). hypozygion aphōnon en anthrōpou phōnē phthengxamenon — a voiceless beast of burden having spoken with a human voice (hypozygion = under-a-yoke, a beast of burden — a donkey; aphōnon = voiceless, unable to speak by nature; the donkey's miraculous speech is the paradox that rebukes the prophet). ekōlusen tēn tou prophētou paraphronian — it restrained the madness of the prophet (paraphronía = madness, being beside one's mind — the word used only here in NT).
17: houtoi eisin pēgai anydroi — these are springs without water (anydros = waterless; the promise of springs — teachers who should provide refreshment — belied by the absence of what they promise). homichlai hypo lailapos elaunomenai — mists/clouds driven by a storm (homichlē = mist, fog; the promise of rain — refreshment for the thirsty — that produces only driven fog). hois ho zophos tou skotous tetērētai — for whom the blackest/murkiest darkness has been preserved (zophos = deep darkness, gloom — the same darkness-word as v.4's chains of darkness).
18: hyperonka gar mataiotētos phthengomenoi — for mouthing great swelling words of emptiness (hyperonkos = over-swollen, inflated — the puffed-up boasting of empty rhetoric; mataiotes = emptiness, futility — the LXX term for hebel/vanity in Ecclesiastes). deleazousin en epithymiais sarkos aselgeiais tous ontōs apopheugontas tous en planē anastraphomenous — they entrap in fleshly desires of licentiousness those who are just escaping from those who live in error. The grammar is complex: they target new converts who have barely escaped the old way of life.
19: eleutherian autois epaggellomenoi — promising freedom to them (epaggellomai = to announce, promise — the language of declaration); douloi hyparchontes tēs phthoras — themselves being slaves of corruption (phthora = corruption/decay — the same word as 1:4's corruption from which believers escape; the false teachers are enslaved to the very thing they claim to offer freedom from). hō gar tis hēttētai, toutō dedoulōtai — by whatever someone is defeated/overcome, to this he has been enslaved (a general principle: defeat in battle produces enslavement; the false teachers are conquered by corruption and therefore serve it).
20: en epignōsei tou Kyriou — through the knowledge of the Lord (epignōsis again — the theme-word of the letter; they had the full knowledge and still returned to the world). toutois de palin emplekomenoi hēttōntai — being entangled again in these and overcome (emplekō = to weave in, entangle — the word for the trap-net closing around prey; hēttaomai = to be worsted, defeated). gegonen autois ta eschata cheirona tōn prōtōn — the last state has become for them worse than the first (echoing Matt 12:45 / Luke 11:26 — the parable of the returning evil spirit).
21: kreitton gar ēn autois mē epegnōkenai tēn hodon tēs dikaiosynēs — for it was better for them not to have known the way of righteousness (epegnōkenai = perfect active infinitive of epiginōskō = to know fully; they had indeed fully known; the knowledge was real but not transformative). ē epignousin epistrepsai ek tēs paradotheisēs autois hagias entolēs — than having known it, to have turned back from the holy commandment delivered to them (paradotheisēs = delivered/handed over — the paradosis/tradition vocabulary; the commandment was received through the apostolic delivery).
22: Two proverbs close the chapter: kyōn epistrepsas epi to idion exerama — a dog having returned to its own vomit (Prov 26:11 LXX — the revulsion of the return to what was once expelled); hys lousamenē eis kylisman borboru — a sow that has washed returning to wallowing in mud (kylisma = a rolling place, a place for wallowing; borboros = mud, mire — a strong word for filth). The two animals contrast clean/unclean: the dog was ritually unclean in Jewish tradition; the pig equally so; both images capture the disgust of apostasy.
